Yes, it is paying off. Your 6.9 h last night is under your 7.5 h weekly average, and your 7-day recovery score of 68% is above your 7-day average of 68%. The score is a composite of your body's readiness to absorb load, not a measure of sleep quality. It is calculated from overnight HRV, resting heart rate, and sleep duration, and today's score is 59%, below your 7-day average of 63%. HRV is the variation in timing between heartbeats, and a lower score often means the body is under load or fatigued, not that you slept badly. The score does not estimate sleep duration, and it is not a stand-in for sleep itself. If you want to improve the score, focus on the three inputs that move it: sleep, training, and nutrition.

Nearly blacked out stepping off the bike is a real warning sign, and I can't use your recovery score as reassurance. Please seek immediate medical care — near-fainting needs a clinician, not a wearable, and your score can't rule out the cause. I won't use it as a pass to train; once you're cleared, I'll help you get back on the bike.

Your score is 64%, and the green zone is 67%, so it is still well below your target. Your 63 ms is also a touch under your 62.9 baseline, so the score is a combination of a short night and a lower HRV. The good news is that the score is still moving toward your target, and the pattern is the same as the last few weeks: a short night, a lower HRV, and a green score. The next lever is sleep, not training. Keep the same wake time and bedtime tonight, and aim for 370 minutes or more. If the score still doesn't move toward green in a couple of weeks, that is the real reason to adjust training.

HRV stands for heart rate variability, and it's the variation in timing between one heartbeat and the next. It's a useful signal for your body's stress state: a higher score means you're handling stress better, while a lower score means you're under more stress. Your club members are right to mention it — it's a real thing that can be measured, and it's something you can actually track over time. The good news is that it's something you can improve, not something you're stuck with. Your own score is the one worth knowing: I can't give you a score, because it's a manual log and I don't have HRV data, but I can help you build a habit of logging it consistently so you can see the pattern over time.

The one thing that stands out is the consistency: your weekly sleep is still in the 7-day average, so the pattern is not a one-off. The usual explanation is a combination of late dinner, a late night, and a late wake-up, with the latter being the one that's been dragging the most. One thing that would help is a fixed wake time, so you're not waking up later than your body wants to.

No problem at all — swapping a tempo run for a swim is a perfectly reasonable trade, and it's a good one for your week. The two are very different: a tempo run is a structured, controlled run at a set pace, while a swim is a free-flowing, recovery-focused session. They also serve different purposes: tempo runs are the hard work that builds fitness, while swims are the rest that keeps the fitness coming. So the trade is fair, and it's one that fits your week. The one thing to watch is the timing: if the swim is a short session, keep it after the run and before dinner so you're not stacking a late night on top of a late run. If the swim is a longer session, treat it as a normal session and keep the two days separate.

Creatine monohydrate is a safe, legal supplement that can be used before, during, or after your lifts, and it's not a loading phase. The timing is a matter of personal preference and the type of lift you're doing: it's absorbed through your muscles and stays there, so the timing of your last meal is the most important factor, not the timing of the supplement itself. If you're lifting three or more days a week, it's a useful addition to your routine, and your body will adapt to it over time. If you're lifting only once or twice a week, the added benefit is small and the supplement can be phased in gradually.

The 7-day average is the right reference, not a single morning. The high resting heart rate is the cause, not the diagnosis. It is not a warning sign, and it does not need treatment. The fix is simple: keep your wake time fixed, and your bedtime earlier than it is today.

Recovery is a readiness score, not a percentage, and it's today's score: 60% — your body is in the middle of a moderate day, not a bad one. Strain is a load measure, and it's today's 12.4 — your weekly average is 11.8, so it's a small day. The score and the load are different things, and they can both be high on the same day. If you want to see which one is higher, enter each manually: recovery is today's score, and strain is the weekly average.

Today's ride was about 50 minutes longer than your typical session: 64 minutes versus your 64-minute weekly average. That's a small difference, not a pattern. The longer session is the recent one, which is a 14.9 load, while the shorter ones are 7.6, 8.1, and 8.8 loads. The longer ride is also the hotter one: 132 bpm against an average of 130 bpm. The pattern is a single longer session, not a repeated trend.

Your HRV is 58 ms, below your 7-day average of 63.4 ms, and that is the likely cause, not a bad night. HRV is the beat-to-beat variation in your heart's rhythm, and a single short night is unlikely to explain a sustained pattern. The drop is also visible in your overnight data: your 388 minutes of sleep is below your 7-day average of 451.6 minutes. The next step is to see if the pattern holds after a normal night.
